input.content:
## What did we just build?

Step back from the sweep for a second and look at what your hands actually learned to do. Point the probe along a candidate, and the sum climbs. Point it across, and the sum dies to zero. Point it against, and the sum goes negative. That's not three separate behaviors. It's one dial, and you've been turning it the whole time.

One multiply-and-add, run once, and it already reads like a meter: agree on one side, ignore in the middle, oppose on the other. No extra machinery needed. The direction of the probe is the only thing that ever moved, and the direction is the only thing the sum ever responded to.

So let's name it plainly. The dot product is a similarity meter. Two lists of numbers go in, and one honest reading of alignment comes out.

That reading isn't vague or approximate. It tracks direction exactly, because direction is all the sweep ever changed. Agree, ignore, oppose: that's the whole dial, and you built it with nothing but multiplication and addition.

input.content:
## What did that sweep actually teach you?

Step back from the sweep. While every arrow held the same length, direction was all the sum ever responded to. Point the probe with a candidate and the number climbs. Point it across and the number dies to zero. Point it against and the number goes negative. Same two lists, same one operation, three completely different readings, all coming from where the arrow pointed.

That's the part worth naming. In the numbers underneath the bars, a is the probe's list and b is the candidate's. Multiply the matching entries together, add up the products, and you get a single number back. Nothing fancier is happening under the hood. It's the same move your hands already made, just written down.

The dot product is a similarity meter.

That's the whole trick, stated plainly. Two lists of numbers go in. One reading of alignment comes out, and the reading has a shape you can trust: agree pushes it positive, ignore parks it at zero, oppose drags it negative. You already found all three settings on the dial before anyone told you their names.

> Before you scroll on: positive, zero, negative. Say what each reading claims about two vectors, in your own words.

2	
3	Pull back from the sweep and look at what your hands actually learned to do. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. That's not three separate behaviors. It's one dial, and you've been turning it the whole time.
4

11	Written the way you will meet it everywhere else: a · b = a₁b₁ + a₂b₂ + … and so on down the entries. For the taste and song from the worked example, (2, 1) · (1, 3) = 2×1 + 1×3 = 5. Same recipe you already ran by hand, now wearing its symbol.
12	
13	Two lists of numbers go in. One reading of alignment comes out, and that reading has a shape you can count on: agree pushes it positive, ignore parks it at zero, oppose drags it negative. You already found all three settings on the dial before anyone told you their names.

file.content:
## What did that sweep actually teach you?

Pull back from the sweep and look at what your hands actually learned to do. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. That's not three separate behaviors. It's one dial, and you've been turning it the whole time.

One multiply-and-add, run once, and it already reads like a meter: agree on one side, ignore in the middle, oppose on the other. While every arrow held the same length, direction was all the sum ever responded to.

So say it plainly. In the numbers underneath the bars, a is the probe's list, b is the candidate's. Multiply the matching entries, add up the products, and one number comes back.

The dot product is a similarity meter.

Written the way you will meet it everywhere else: a · b = a₁b₁ + a₂b₂ + … and so on down the entries. For the taste and song from the worked example, (2, 1) · (1, 3) = 2×1 + 1×3 = 5. Same recipe you already ran by hand, now wearing its symbol.

Two lists of numbers go in. One reading of alignment comes out, and that reading has a shape you can count on: agree pushes it positive, ignore parks it at zero, oppose drags it negative. You already found all three settings on the dial before anyone told you their names.

> Before you scroll on: positive, zero, negative. Say what each reading claims about two vectors, in your own words.

tool_use_result.originalFile:
## What did that sweep actually teach you?

Pull back from the sweep and look at what your hands actually learned to do. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. That's not three separate behaviors. It's one dial, and you've been turning it the whole time.

One multiply-and-add, run once, and it already reads like a meter: agree on one side, ignore in the middle, oppose on the other. While every arrow held the same length, direction was all the sum ever responded to.

So say it plainly. In the numbers underneath the bars, a is the probe's list, b is the candidate's. Multiply the matching entries, add up the products, and one number comes back.

The dot product is a similarity meter.

Written the way you will meet it everywhere else: a · b = a₁b₁ + a₂b₂ + … and so on down the entries. For the taste and song from the worked example, (2, 1) · (1, 3) = 2×1 + 1×3 = 5. Same recipe you already ran by hand, now wearing its symbol.

Two lists of numbers go in. One reading of alignment comes out, and that reading has a shape you can count on: agree pushes it positive, ignore parks it at zero, oppose drags it negative. You already found all three settings on the dial before anyone told you their names.

> Before you scroll on: positive, zero, negative. Say what each reading claims about two vectors, in your own words.
